findings.title: — No hardcoded secrets or credentials
findings.description: The API key is hardcoded in the source code, which exposes it to potential security risks. Hardcoded secrets should be avoided and instead retrieved from environment variables or a secret manager.

findings.title: — Never store context in a struct
findings.description: Storing context in a struct field leads to lifecycle bugs. Contexts are request-scoped and should be passed as a function parameter.

findings.title: SEVERITY — Always wrap errors with context
findings.description: The error returned from `readFile` is not wrapped with `fmt.Errorf`, which loses the call chain context.

findings.title: SEVERITY — Never ignore errors
findings.description: Ignoring error return with `_` leads to silent failures that are extremely hard to debug in production.

findings.title: SEVERITY — No panic in library or service code
findings.description: The use of `panic` for a recoverable error condition is not allowed. It crashes the entire process and cannot be handled gracefully by callers.

findings.title: — Use structured logging only
findings.description: The code uses `fmt.Println` for logging, which is not structured and does not include timestamps, levels, or other useful information.

findings.title: Pre-allocate slices when size is known
findings.description: The slice `names` is not pre-allocated, which can lead to repeated allocations and copying during growth.

findings.title: — Use table-driven tests
findings.description: The test does not use the table-driven pattern, making it less readable and harder to extend.

findings.title: — Exported functions must have doc comments
findings.description: The function `ProcessOrder` is exported but has no doc comment. This violates the rule that every exported function must have a doc comment.

findings.title: Use MixedCaps for Go names, not underscores
findings.description: The variable `names` uses an underscore in its name, which is not idiomatic in Go.
